He did the opposite: he continued advocating for women's suffrage in lectures, writings, and political appearances for the remaining twenty-six years of his life.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Frederick attended to the structural distinction between coalition tactical position (sequenced versus simultaneous enfranchisement) and personal advocacy position (women's suffrage as a political goal). He noticed that the AERA rupture had been over the former, not the latter — Stanton and Anthony had not denied that women's suffrage was a goal; they had insisted on simultaneous tactical demand. He noticed that withdrawing from women's suffrage advocacy entirely would conflate the two positions and would functionally ratify the framing that coalition disagreement required position abandonment.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Frederick's operative goals were: (1) preserve his personal advocacy for women's suffrage independent of the coalition rupture; (2) refuse to let coalition disagreement determine position-level commitment; (3) continue to be present at women's suffrage events as a public advocate, even when those events were organized by the rival NWSA faction; (4) maintain the example of decoupled advocacy as a pedagogical signal for movement participants who might otherwise read coalition rupture as requiring position abandonment.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Frederick rejected the alternative of withdrawing from women's suffrage advocacy after the rupture, which would have conflated tactical and position-level disagreement. He rejected the alternative of advocating only at AWSA-affiliated events, which would have institutionalized the rupture into his own conduct. He rejected the alternative of using continued advocacy to attempt reconciliation with Stanton and Anthony at the cost of his analytical position on the sequencing question; the advocacy was independent of the reconciliation question.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Frederick framed his post-rupture conduct as the public demonstration that coalition disagreement and position-level advocacy were separable instruments. The frame allowed him to disagree with Stanton and Anthony on tactical sequencing while continuing to share platforms with women's suffrage advocates and to write in support of the cause. The frame is the same operational decoupling visible across his career: positions and alliances are separable variables that can be managed independently rather than collapsed into one another.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Frederick expected to continue advocating for women's suffrage at every available platform; to attend women's suffrage events including those organized by NWSA; to gradually rebuild personal relations with Stanton and Anthony as the immediate heat of the rupture cooled (partial reconciliation occurred over the 1880s); and to remain a publicly identified women's suffrage advocate throughout his life. He expected his February 20, 1895 attendance at a women's suffrage meeting on the morning of his death (which occurred) to be a closing demonstration that the position had been continuous despite the coalition rupture.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another senior abolitionist who had ruptured with Stanton and Anthony — would in most cases have either withdrawn from women's suffrage advocacy entirely (treating the rupture as comprehensive) or rebuilt the coalition through tactical compromise (subordinating the operational analysis to coalition coherence). Few peers would have selected the path of continued personal advocacy without coalition reconciliation, which required holding the public position over decades without the institutional infrastructure of either suffrage organization.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Frederick's divergence rests on his consistent treatment of position and coalition as separable instruments. He refuses to let coalition rupture determine position; he refuses to let coalition coherence determine position; the position is determined by his analytical assessment of the goal, and the coalition relationship is a separate instrument that can be reconfigured. The same disposition is visible in his maintenance of public advocacy for Reconstruction policy after the Hayes administration's betrayal, in his continued support of Republican administrations whose racial policies he criticized, and in his post-resignation defense of Haitian sovereignty.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Frederick continued advocating for women's suffrage until his death on February 20, 1895. He attended a women's suffrage meeting at the National Council of Women on that morning, returned to his Cedar Hill home in Anacostia, and died of a heart attack that evening. His final public act was advocacy for women's suffrage. The decoupled-advocacy model held: he was present at the cause's events for twenty-six years after the coalition rupture, and his presence helped sustain the AWSA wing of the movement and eventually contributed to the partial reconciliation of the two organizations into the National American Woman Suffrage Association in 1890.

critical_incident_database.decision: On January 2, 1893, after his resignation from the Haiti ministership, Frederick delivered the 'Lecture on Haiti' in Boston, publicly defending the Haitian government's refusal of the Môle lease against the prevailing U.S. characterization that the refusal had reflected anti-American sentiment.
critical_incident_database.context: Following his July 1891 resignation from the Haiti post, the State Department had publicly attributed the failure of the Môle negotiation partly to insufficient pressure from Frederick, and elements of the U.S. press had characterized Haiti's refusal as evidence of anti-American sentiment or political instability. By 1893, the Chicago World's Fair was approaching, and Haiti had appointed Frederick as its honorary commissioner — the only black commissioner of any country at the fair. Frederick used the Boston lecture to publicly defend Haitian sovereignty against the U.S. characterization.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Frederick attended to the structural fact that the bounded-execution model he had used at the post required a post-resignation public-defense channel to function as advertised. He noticed that without the public-defense, the bounded-execution would be retroactively read as either failure (insufficient pressure) or covert undermining (improper sympathy with the host government); only the public-defense after resignation could clarify what bounded execution had actually meant. He noticed that the Boston lecture, delivered to a sympathetic audience in a major venue, was the appropriate platform for the defense.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Frederick's operative goals were: (1) publicly defend Haitian sovereignty against the U.S. characterization, on the recognition that the characterization was substantively incorrect and that the silence of the post's former U.S. minister would consolidate it; (2) clarify the bounded-execution model retroactively, by demonstrating that the disagreement with U.S. policy had been preserved rather than absorbed; (3) refuse to leak State Department confidences during the defense, which would have terminated his moral standing; (4) align himself with Haiti's appointment of him as World's Fair commissioner, supporting the relationship that had been preserved despite the negotiation's failure.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Frederick rejected the alternative of remaining silent on Haiti after resignation, which would have allowed the State Department's characterization to consolidate without contest and would have retroactively compromised the bounded-execution model. He rejected the alternative of using the lecture to leak State Department communications or to characterize specific U.S. officials, which would have crossed the discretion-boundary and would not have been operationally necessary; the substantive defense of Haitian sovereignty did not require disclosure of U.S. internal positions. He rejected the alternative of softening the defense with diplomatic hedges, which would have weakened the substantive position; the defense had to be substantively grounded.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Frederick framed the lecture as the post-resignation completion of the bounded-execution model. The model had three components — accept the post; execute honestly within bounds; publicly defend the host government's position after resignation — and the lecture executed the third component. The frame allowed him to deploy his independent voice on Haitian sovereignty without violating any commitment he had made during his service. The lecture was structurally part of the post's design, not an addition to it.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Frederick expected the lecture to circulate widely (it did — it was published as a pamphlet and excerpted in the antislavery and reform press); to be criticized by State Department-aligned observers (it was); to consolidate the relationship with Haiti that had survived the negotiation's failure (it did — Haiti's appointment of him as World's Fair commissioner was the operational expression); to reinforce the bounded-execution model as a viable template for subsequent black diplomatic appointees (the model was rarely tested at the same level afterward, but the precedent was preserved). He expected to absorb continuing State Department criticism as the cost of the public defense.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another former diplomat who had resigned a contentious post — would in most cases have either remained publicly silent on the underlying policy disagreement (preserving discretion at the cost of historical record correction) or attacked the State Department's handling of the post (preserving the analytical position at the cost of discretion-boundary violation). Few peers would have selected the substantive-defense-without-leakage path, which required defending the foreign government's sovereignty position on its merits without disclosing U.S. internal positions.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Frederick's divergence rests on his consistent maintenance of separable channels for public criticism and operational confidentiality. He executes the role honestly during service; he defends the host government's position publicly after resignation; he refuses to mix the two by leaking confidences during service or by hedging the substantive defense after resignation. The same disposition is visible in his Lincoln-era management of public criticism alongside private cooperation, and in his Reconstruction-era management of patronage acceptance alongside policy criticism.
critical_incident_database.outcome: The 'Lecture on Haiti' was delivered at Quinn Chapel A.M.E. Church in Chicago and at the Boston Theatre and was published as a pamphlet. It became a foundational text in the African-American intellectual tradition of pan-Africanist solidarity. Haiti's appointment of Frederick as World's Fair commissioner was operationalized at the 1893 Columbian Exposition, where Frederick used the Haitian Pavilion as a platform for further commentary on U.S. racial politics and on the structural conditions of black political participation. The bounded-execution model was retroactively vindicated by the public defense's success at clarifying its operational meaning.

critical_incident_database.decision: On adopting his new surname after escape, Frederick selected 'Douglass' from Walter Scott's 'The Lady of the Lake' on the suggestion of his New Bedford host Nathan Johnson, accepting a literary-romantic nominal identity rather than retaining 'Bailey' or selecting a more politically resonant name.
critical_incident_database.context: On arriving in New Bedford, Massachusetts in late September 1838, Frederick (then bearing the slave name 'Frederick Augustus Washington Bailey') stayed initially with Nathan Johnson, a free black abolitionist. The fugitive needed a new surname for both safety and identity reasons. Johnson had been reading Scott's narrative poem 'The Lady of the Lake' and proposed 'Douglass' (the protagonist of the poem). Frederick accepted. The decision determined the public name under which he would conduct the rest of his life's work.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Frederick attended to the structural function of a new name as both an operational instrument (legal identity protecting against fugitive recapture) and a symbolic instrument (signal of the new political identity he was constructing). He noticed that the name had to serve both functions: it had to be sufficiently common to provide cover and sufficiently distinctive to carry symbolic weight. He noticed that selecting a politically resonant name (e.g., a founder's name, an abolitionist's name) would commit him to a specific political alignment before he had fully constructed his role; a literary-romantic name allowed the political identity to be developed independent of the nominal one.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Frederick's operative goals were: (1) acquire a new legal identity that would protect against fugitive recapture; (2) signal a new political identity without committing to a specific alignment in advance; (3) defer to Johnson's hospitality and judgment in a moment when he was still constructing his free-side networks; (4) avoid a name whose symbolic content would compete with the substantive role he intended to construct; (5) preserve the option of altering the symbolic content of the name through subsequent action rather than by selection.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Frederick rejected the alternative of retaining 'Bailey,' which would have preserved the slave-name and made operational identification by fugitive-recovery agents straightforward. He rejected the alternative of selecting a politically resonant name (e.g., 'Garrison,' 'Phillips,' a founder's name), which would have committed him to a specific alignment before his role was developed. He rejected the alternative of selecting a name with biographical specificity to his own family or origins, which would have given fugitive-recovery agents a research thread.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Frederick framed the name selection as a structural-identity construction problem rather than as a personal-identity question. The frame allowed him to defer to Johnson's literary suggestion while preserving the symbolic content for later construction through his own actions. The frame is unusual: most fugitive name-selections were either purely defensive (any non-distinctive surname) or purely symbolic (a politically chosen name). The literary-romantic option was a third path that allowed both functions to be served without forcing a premature commitment.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Frederick expected the new surname to provide adequate cover under the federal Fugitive Slave Act of 1793 (it did, until the Narrative's publication in 1845 made the surname publicly identifiable to his enslaver); to serve as a recognizable public identity once he began the lecture circuit (it did); to acquire whatever symbolic content his subsequent actions imparted to it (it did — by 1865 'Douglass' had become a recognized public name with substantial political weight); and to avoid committing him to alignments his role might later require him to revise (it did).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another fugitive arriving in a free-state household with the same name-selection problem — would in many cases have either retained the slave-name (preserving continuity at the cost of operational risk) or selected a politically resonant name (committing to alignment in advance). Few peers would have selected a literary-romantic name on the recommendation of a host without further deliberation. The selection's operational adequacy was partly a matter of luck — Scott's poem provided a ready-to-hand surname that turned out to function well — and Frederick's adoption of it without prolonged deliberation reflected an appropriate distribution of decision-effort across high-stakes and lower-stakes moments.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Frederick's divergence is more modest in this incident than in others; the decision was partly delegated to Johnson, and the operational and symbolic functions of the name were both adequately served by the literary-romantic selection. The pattern of CDM interest is the willingness to defer to local hospitality and judgment in a moment of high uncertainty rather than to attempt full optimization, combined with the awareness that the symbolic content of a name is constructed by subsequent action rather than by selection. The pattern is consistent with his career-long preference for letting facts on the ground rather than nominal commitments determine alignment.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Frederick used the surname 'Douglass' for the remaining fifty-seven years of his life. The name acquired substantial symbolic content through his subsequent actions: Narrative author, North Star editor, Lincoln's interlocutor, Reconstruction-era political figure, diplomatic appointee, women's suffrage advocate. By the time of his death in 1895, the surname was sufficiently established that no further symbolic content was required from external sources; it had been built entirely through his own work.

critical_incident_database.decision: Across his thirty-year tenure delivering 'Self-Made Men' to mixed audiences, Frederick refused to deliver differential versions of the lecture for white and black audiences, articulating instead a single doctrine of achievement that applied symmetrically and treating black exemplars as instances of the universal standard rather than as exceptions to it.
critical_incident_database.context: Frederick first delivered 'Self-Made Men' in March 1859 and continued delivering it more than fifty times over the following thirty years. The lecture became his most-delivered public address and articulated his most sustained philosophy of achievement. Throughout the lecture's history, Frederick faced periodic suggestions from organizers and from sympathetic correspondents that he deliver a modified version for black audiences, with explicit discussion of racial constraints, and a different version for white audiences, without that material. He refused.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Frederick attended to the structural significance of differential standards. He noticed that articulating a separate 'achievement under racial constraint' framework for black audiences would conceptually ratify the premise that achievement standards differ by race, and that the ratification would be a structural concession he was unwilling to make. He noticed that black exemplars (Toussaint Louverture, himself implicitly, others in the lecture's enumeration) functioned as instances of the universal standard if the universal standard was articulated symmetrically; they functioned as exceptions if the standard was differentiated.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Frederick's operative goals were: (1) articulate a single achievement standard that applied symmetrically to all subjects; (2) refuse the conceptual space for differential racial standards by simply not constructing differentiated content; (3) treat black exemplars as instances of the universal standard, requiring no special pleading; (4) preserve the lecture's pedagogical function for black audiences by making the standard operationally accessible rather than rhetorically inflated; (5) refuse the rhetorical strategy of separate-and-equal racial framings even when offered as well-intentioned suggestions.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Frederick rejected the alternative of delivering modified versions for different audiences, which would have ratified differential standards. He rejected the alternative of inserting explicit racial-constraint material into the lecture for black audiences, which would have shifted the lecture's analytical frame from achievement-doctrine to racial-circumstance commentary. He rejected the alternative of identifying himself explicitly as an exemplar within the lecture (he uses himself implicitly through the doctrine's autobiographical resonance, not explicitly through self-citation), which would have undermined the universal-standard framing.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Frederick framed the lecture as a universal-achievement doctrine that applied symmetrically across racial categories, and treated the operational test of the framing as whether the lecture could be delivered to any audience without modification. The frame required him to refuse the seemingly accommodating gesture of delivering modified versions; the modification would have undermined the universal-standard claim. The frame is the same as his refusal of differential racial standards in his marriage's public defense and as his treatment of his own autobiographies as universal-historical record rather than as racially-specific testimony.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Frederick expected the lecture to function differently for different audiences but with the same content: white audiences would hear a generic philosophy of achievement; black audiences would hear a specifically applicable philosophy. He expected the doctrine's pedagogical utility to compound across both audience types over decades. He expected the refusal of differential versions to be occasionally criticized as excessive symmetrical-treatment that ignored real racial differences in achievement conditions, and he expected to defend the refusal on the grounds that the symmetrical treatment was itself the structural intervention.

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another black orator with a developed achievement-doctrine — would in many cases have delivered modified versions for different audiences, on the reasoning that audience-specific framing produced better pedagogical outcomes. The modification would have been tactically defensible but would have ratified differential standards. Few peers would have refused the modification explicitly on structural grounds and articulated the refusal as itself part of the doctrine.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Frederick's divergence rests on his structural treatment of conceptual categories. He refuses to ratify differential racial standards even when the ratification would be tactically convenient, because the ratification is itself the structural concession he is opposing. The pattern is the same as his refusal to negotiate with white abolitionist movements that placed black testimony in service of white argumentation: the structural implication of the differentiation is the principal stake, not the immediate pedagogical convenience.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Frederick delivered 'Self-Made Men' approximately fifty times across thirty years to mixed audiences, with periodic content revisions but without audience-differentiated versions. The doctrine became the canonical articulation of his achievement philosophy and circulated through pamphlet publication and excerpts in the press. The single-standard framing influenced subsequent black political philosophy (visible in Booker T. Washington's later work, though with different operational implications) and remains the most-cited expression of Frederick's mature philosophical position.

critical_incident_database.decision: During the 1864 reelection campaign, Frederick declined to publicly criticize Lincoln on the residual policy issues he had unresolved, calculating that the public criticism would be operationally counterproductive in the immediate electoral context even though private criticism remained appropriate.
critical_incident_database.context: By summer 1864, Frederick had unresolved policy disagreements with the Lincoln administration on multiple issues: commissioning of black officers (slow), retaliation policy (partial), reconstruction planning (Lincoln's ten-percent plan was insufficient), and others. Lincoln's reelection prospects appeared dire. McClellan's Democratic platform called for negotiated peace. Frederick understood that public criticism of Lincoln during the reelection campaign would aid McClellan's candidacy, and McClellan's victory would produce a worse outcome than Lincoln's residual policy gaps.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Frederick attended to the structural fact that the 1864 election was a binary outcome between Lincoln and McClellan, and that the policy gaps between Lincoln and Frederick's preferred positions were quantitatively smaller than the policy gaps between Lincoln and McClellan. He noticed that public criticism of Lincoln during the campaign would aid McClellan's candidacy; that Frederick's voice was audible enough to make the aid operationally significant; and that the criticism could be deployed publicly after the election with substantially less downside.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Frederick's operative goals were: (1) maximize the probability of Lincoln's reelection, on the recognition that the alternative was structurally worse; (2) preserve the policy criticism for post-election deployment; (3) maintain private channels for criticism through correspondence with Lincoln, Sumner, Andrew, and others; (4) avoid the appearance of full endorsement that would compromise post-election independence; (5) refuse to let his analytical voice be used as an instrument of the McClellan campaign's argumentation.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Frederick rejected the alternative of publicly criticizing Lincoln on the residual policy issues during the campaign, which would have aided McClellan and would have been operationally counterproductive. He rejected the alternative of full public endorsement of Lincoln without reservation, which would have ratified Lincoln's policy positions and compromised post-election criticism. He rejected the alternative of full public silence, which could have been read as withdrawal of support and would not have provided the operational endorsement the campaign required from a senior black political voice. He selected a calibrated public posture: support for Lincoln's reelection without full endorsement of all administration positions, with criticism deferred.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Frederick framed the campaign-period criticism question as a temporal-deployment question rather than as a content question. The criticism was substantively correct and would be deployed; the only question was when. Public deployment during the campaign aided McClellan; private deployment to Lincoln himself was operationally available; public deployment after the election was strategically optimal. The frame allowed him to maintain the analytical position without sacrificing the electoral calculation.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Frederick expected Lincoln's reelection to occur (it did, decisively, after Sherman's Atlanta capture); the residual policy issues to be addressable post-election (some were addressed during the lame-duck period, including the 13th Amendment passage; others remained unresolved and were addressed through Reconstruction-era advocacy); his own criticism to be deployable publicly post-election without compromising his support for the administration during the campaign (it was).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another senior black abolitionist with policy disagreements with Lincoln — would in some cases have either publicly criticized Lincoln during the campaign (privileging the analytical position over the electoral calculation) or fully endorsed Lincoln (privileging the electoral calculation at the cost of the analytical position). Few peers would have selected the calibrated path of supporting reelection without full endorsement, with criticism deferred.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Frederick's divergence rests on his consistent treatment of public criticism as a temporal-deployment instrument whose timing is selectable separately from its content. The criticism's substantive correctness does not require its immediate public deployment; the deployment can be timed for maximum operational impact. The pattern is the same as his post-Lincoln-meeting public/private channel decoupling and as his post-Hayes-marshalship continuation of public Reconstruction criticism alongside operational service.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Frederick supported Lincoln's reelection without full endorsement during the 1864 campaign and resumed public policy criticism after the November election. He met with Lincoln again on March 4, 1865 at the post-Inaugural reception (the famous incident in which Lincoln greeted him in the receiving line as 'my friend Douglass'). The post-election deployment of his policy criticism was operationally available and was used to advocate for the 13th Amendment's passage and for the structural improvements to black soldier treatment that the lame-duck and post-war periods enabled. The temporal-deployment model held.

critical_incident_database.decision: After arriving in New York in September 1838 with no resources and a precarious legal status, Frederick chose to relocate from New York City to New Bedford, Massachusetts within days, on the calculation that New York's high concentration of fugitive-recovery agents and informers made it a structurally hostile operational environment despite its advantages.
critical_incident_database.context: Frederick had arrived in New York City via train on the morning of September 4, 1838, after a successful single-actor escape from Baltimore. He had limited funds, no shelter, and the federal Fugitive Slave Act of 1793 in active enforcement. New York City contained the largest concentration of free black community in the North, but it also contained a substantial population of fugitive-recovery agents, informers, and slave-catchers operating under federal protection. David Ruggles, a free black abolitionist in New York, advised Frederick to relocate to New Bedford, where the maritime economy provided employment and the free-black community was more cohesive against fugitive-recovery operations.

cdm_probes.cues_noticed: Frederick attended to the structural difference between aggregate population and operational hostility. He noticed that New York's larger free-black population was offset by a larger and more active fugitive-recovery infrastructure, and that the relevant variable was not population size but the ratio of supportive to hostile actors in the operational environment. He noticed that Ruggles's advice came from someone with detailed operational knowledge of the New York fugitive-recovery infrastructure and could be trusted on the structural analysis.
cdm_probes.active_goals: Frederick's operative goals were: (1) establish a stable operational environment in which legal exposure to fugitive-recovery was minimized; (2) acquire employment that would support Anna Murray's joining him and their starting a family; (3) build connections with the abolitionist community on terms compatible with eventual movement participation; (4) accept the recommendation of an experienced free-black advisor without prolonged independent deliberation; (5) preserve the option of subsequent relocation if the New Bedford selection proved inadequate.
cdm_probes.rejected_alternatives: Frederick rejected the alternative of remaining in New York City, on the recognition that the higher operational hostility outweighed the larger aggregate community. He rejected the alternative of continuing further north (Boston, Albany), on the recognition that further travel without rest and without employment would consume resources and could attract attention. He rejected the alternative of attempting to move directly to Canada, which would have placed him beyond U.S. fugitive-recovery jurisdiction at the cost of removing him from the U.S. operational environment where his work was located. He rejected the alternative of conducting independent investigation of the relocation question rather than deferring to Ruggles's advice; the deferral was operationally appropriate given his lack of local knowledge.
cdm_probes.situation_framing: Frederick framed the relocation as a tactical operational decision under conditions of precarious legal status, in which deference to local advisors with detailed operational knowledge was the appropriate decision-making mode. The frame allowed him to make the relocation rapidly without prolonged deliberation and to preserve cognitive resources for the larger architectural decisions (marriage, employment, abolitionist movement engagement) that would follow. The frame is the same as the New Bedford name-selection deference to Johnson: high-stakes-but-locally-informed decisions are appropriately delegated to local advisors without compromising the larger architectural agenda.
cdm_probes.expected_outcome: Frederick expected New Bedford to provide employment in the maritime caulking industry (it did); to allow Anna Murray to join him within weeks (she did, and they were married on September 15, 1838); to provide a base from which abolitionist movement engagement could be initiated when opportunity arose (it did, three years later at Nantucket); to keep him beyond the most active fugitive-recovery operations of New York City (it did, with the recovery threat deferred until the Narrative's publication in 1845).

critical_incident_database.counterfactual: A competent peer — another fugitive arriving in New York with similar resources — would in many cases have either remained in New York City (privileging community size over operational hostility) or attempted further travel to Canada (privileging legal safety over U.S. operational location). Few peers would have selected New Bedford on the rapid recommendation of a local advisor without prolonged independent deliberation; the rapid relocation under deferred judgment was Frederick's distinctive operational mode at moments where local knowledge was the binding constraint.
critical_incident_database.divergence_explanation: Frederick's divergence rests on his rapid distribution of decision-effort: high-architectural decisions receive prolonged independent deliberation; high-stakes-but-locally-informed decisions are appropriately delegated to local advisors and executed rapidly. The pattern requires accurate identification of which decisions belong in which category, and the New Bedford relocation is an instance of correct categorization — operational tactical decision under local-information constraint, appropriate to delegate.
critical_incident_database.outcome: Frederick relocated to New Bedford within five days of arriving in New York. He found work as a caulker and as a manual laborer; Anna Murray joined him; they were married on September 15, 1838; they remained in New Bedford for three years before his recruitment to the Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society's lecture circuit in October 1841. The New Bedford period provided the operational stability and economic base that the subsequent lecture-circuit decision required. The relocation's correctness was vindicated by the absence of fugitive-recovery activity against him during the New Bedford years.
